content: What should be the resistance of an ideal ammeter and an ideal voltmeter?
explanation: Resistance of an ideal ammeter is zero. And resistance of an ideal voltmeter is infinity.

content: A particle of charge ' $q$ ' and mass ' $m$ ' moves in a circular orbit of radius ' $r$ ' with angular speed ' $\omega$ '. The ratio of the magnitude of its magnetic moment to that of its angular momentum depends on
explanation:
The magnetic moment is

$$\mu = I\,A = \frac{q\omega}{2\pi}\times(\pi r^2)=\frac{q\omega r^2}{2},$$

and the angular momentum is

$$L = m r^2\omega.$$

Thus

$$\frac{\mu}{L}=\frac{\frac{q\omega r^2}{2}}{m r^2\omega}=\frac{q}{2m},$$

which depends only on $q$ and $m$. (Option C)

content: A charged particle moving with a velocity $$\vec{v}=v_1 \hat{i}+v_2 \hat{j}$$ in a magnetic field $$\vec{B}$$ experiences a force $$\vec{F}=F_1 \hat{i}+F_2 \hat{j}$$. Here $$v_1, v_2, F_1, F_2$$ all are constants. Then $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$$ can be
explanation:
Let the charge on particle is q.

Given,

$$\overrightarrow V = {V_1}\widehat i + {V_2}\widehat j$$

$$\overrightarrow F = {F_1}\widehat i + {F_2}\widehat j$$

Let's assume that magnetic field is

$$\overrightarrow B = {B_1}\widehat i + {B_2}\widehat j + {B_3}\widehat k$$

We know that,

$$\overrightarrow F = q(\overrightarrow v \times \overrightarrow B )\quad \text{... (i)}$$

Let's find, $$\overrightarrow v \times \overrightarrow B $$, by cross product

$$\overrightarrow v \times \overrightarrow B = \left| {\matrix{ {\widehat i} & {\widehat j} & {\widehat j} \cr {{v_1}} & {{v_2}} & 0 \cr {{B_1}} & {{B_2}} & {{B_3}} \cr } } \right|$$

$$ = {v_2}{B_3}\widehat i - {v_1}{B_3}\widehat j + ({v_1}{B_2} - {v_2}{B_1})\widehat k$$

Hence, by eq. (i),

$${F_1}\widehat i + {F_2}\widehat j = q{v_2}{B_3}\widehat i - q{v_1}{B_3}\widehat j + q({v_1}{B_2} - {v_2}{B_1})\widehat k$$

So we can write,

$$q({v_1}{B_2} - {v_2}{B_1}) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {v_1}{B_2} - {v_2}{B_1} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}} = {{{B_1}} \over {{B_2}}}$$

Hence, option (B) is correct.

content: A wire carrying a steady current I is kept in the x-y plane along the curve $$y=A \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} x\right)$$. A magnetic field B exists in the z-direction. The magnitude of the magnetic force in the portion of the wire between x = 0 and x = $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
The magnetic force on a current carrying wire is

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathbf{F} ={I l} \times \mathbf{B}={I} \lambda \times \mathbf{B} \\ \therefore \quad & F =I \lambda B \sin 90^{\circ}=I \lambda B \end{aligned} $$

content: A bar magnet falls from rest under gravity through the centre of a horizontal ring of conducting wire as shown in figure. Which of the following graph best represents the speed (v) vs. time (t) graph of the bar magnet?
explanation:
Initially only gravity force acts on the magnet.

$$\therefore$$ The speed of magnet increases from zero to certain value. When the magnet reaches near the ring, then opposing magnetic force $$F_m$$ acts on the magnet. So, its speed decreases.

Similarly when magnet crosses the ring and goes away, then only gravity force acts and the speed of magnet increases.

content: Two infinite line-charges parallel to each other are moving with a constant velocity v in the same direction as shown in the figure. The separation between two line-charges is d. The magnetic attraction balances the electric repulsion when, [ c = speed of light in free space ]
explanation:
Electric field due to line charge is given by

$$E = {\lambda \over {2\pi {\varepsilon _0}d}}$$

where, $$\lambda$$ = linear charge density.

$$\lambda = {q \over l} \Rightarrow q = \lambda l$$

Electric force due to one wire on another wire,

$${F_E} = q\,.\,E$$

$$ = \lambda l\,.\,{\lambda \over {2\pi {\varepsilon _0}d}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {F_E} = {{{\lambda ^2}l} \over {2\pi {\varepsilon _0}d}}$$

Now, current due to moving charged wire is given as

$$I = {{dq} \over {dt}} = {d \over {dt}}(\lambda dl)$$ [$$\because$$ $$v = {{dl} \over {dt}}$$]

$$ = \lambda v$$

Magnetic force on one wire due to another wire,

$${F_B} = {{{\mu _0}{I_1}{I_2}l} \over {2\pi d}}$$

Since, both forces balance each other, thus

$${F_E} = {F_B}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{\lambda ^2}l} \over {2\pi {\varepsilon _0}d}} = {{{\mu _0}(\lambda v)(\lambda v)l} \over {2\pi d}}$$ [$$\because$$ $${I_1} = {I_2} = I$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {{\varepsilon _0}{\mu _0}}} = {v^2}$$

or, $$v = {1 \over {\sqrt {{\varepsilon _0}{\mu _0}} }} = c$$

content: An electron revolves around the nucleus in a circular path with angular momentum $$\overrightarrow L $$. A uniform magnetic field $$\overrightarrow B $$ is applied perpendicular to the plane of its orbit. If the electron experiences a torque $$\overrightarrow T $$, then

options.content: Angle between $$\overrightarrow T $$ and $$\overrightarrow L $$ is $$45^\circ $$

explanation:
According to the question, given situation can be represented by the figure below.

From the Lorentz force, we have

$$F = q(v \times B)$$

Hence, force is acting directly towards the centre.

Thus, both angular momentum ($$\mathbf{L}$$) and torque ($$\mathbf{T}$$) will have the same direction. i.e. $$\mathbf{T}$$ || $$\mathbf{L}$$.

content: A straight wire is placed in a magnetic field that varies with distance x from origin as $$\overrightarrow B = {B_0}\left( {2 - {x \over a}} \right)\widehat k$$. Ends of wire are at (a, 0) and (2a, 0) and it carries a current I. If force on wire is $$\overrightarrow F = I{B_0}\left( {{{ka} \over 2}} \right)\widehat j$$, then value of k is
explanation:
Given, $$B = {B_0}\left( {2 - {x \over a}} \right)\widehat k$$

Force on a current carrying wire is given as

$$dF = IB\,.\,dl$$

$$ \Rightarrow \int {dF = \int {I{B_0}\left( {2 - {x \over a}} \right)dx} } $$

$$ \Rightarrow F = I{B_0}\int\limits_a^{2a} {\left( {2 - {x \over a}} \right)dx} $$

$$ = I{B_0}\left| {2x - {{{x^2}} \over {2a}}} \right|_a^{2a} = I{B_0}\left( {{a \over 2}} \right)$$

Therefore, the value of k is 1.

content: A horizontal semi-circular wire of radius r is connected to a battery through two similar springs X and Y to an electric cell, which sends current I through it. A vertically downward uniform magnetic field B is applied on the wire, as shown in the figure. What is the force acting on each spring?
explanation:
Considering the semi-circular wire of radius r. Force acting on it is shown below

Let T be the force exerted by each spring.

$$\therefore$$ $$T + T = IBl$$

$$2T = IBl$$

$$2T = IB\,.\,\pi r$$ ($$\because$$ $$l = \pi r$$)

$$\therefore$$ $$T = {{IB\pi r} \over 2}$$

content: Consider two infinitely long wires parallel to Z-axis carrying same current I in the positive z-direction. One wire passes through the point L at coordinates ($$-$$1, +1) and the other wire passes through the point M at coordinates ($$-$$1, $$-$$1). The resultant magnetic field at the origin O will be
explanation:
The given situation is shown below


Length, $$OL = \sqrt {{1^2} + {{( - 1)}^2}} = \sqrt 2 $$

and $$OM = \sqrt {{{( - 1)}^2} + {{( - 1)}^2}} = \sqrt 2 $$

$$\because$$ Magnetic field at point O due to wire L,

$${B_L} = {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.{I \over {\sqrt 2 }}\left( {{{\widehat i + \widehat j} \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)$$ $$\because$$ $$\left[ {B = {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.{I \over r}} \right]$$

Magnetic field at point O due to wire M,

$${B_M} = {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.{I \over {\sqrt 2 }}\left( {{{ - \widehat i + \widehat j} \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ Resultant magnetic field at the origin O,

$$B = {B_L} + {B_M}$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {2\pi \sqrt 2 }}\left( {{{\widehat i + \widehat j} \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right) + {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}.{I \over {\sqrt 2 }}\left( {{{ - \widehat i + \widehat j} \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {2\sqrt 2 \pi }}\left[ {{{\widehat i + \widehat j - \widehat i + \widehat j} \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right]$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {2\sqrt 2 }}.(\sqrt 2 \widehat j) = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {2\pi }}\widehat j$$

content: A thin charged rod is bent into the shape of a small circle of radius R, the charge per unit length of the rod being $$\lambda$$. The circle is rotated about its axis with a time period T and it is found the magnetic field at a distance d away (d >> R) from the centre and on the axis, varies as $$R^m \over d^n$$. The values of m and n respectively are
explanation:
Charge per unit length of the thin rod = $$\lambda$$

If l be the length of the rod, the charge on the rod,

q = l$$\lambda$$ ..... (i)

According to given situation,

l = 2$$\pi$$R

$$\therefore$$ From Eq. (i), we get

q = 2$$\pi$$R$$\lambda$$

Current associated,

$$I = {q \over T}$$

$$I = {{2\pi R\lambda } \over T}$$ ..... (ii)

Magnetic field on the axis at a distance d away from the centre,

$$B = {{{\mu _0}I{R^2}} \over {2{{({R^2} + {d^2})}^{3/2}}}}$$

For d >> R,

$$B = {{{\mu _0}I{R^2}} \over {2{{({d^2})}^{3/2}}}}$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}I{R^2}} \over {2{d^3}}}$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}\left( {{{2\pi R\lambda } \over T}} \right){R^2}} \over {2{d^3}}}$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}\pi \lambda } \over T}.{{{R^3}} \over {{d^3}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow B \propto {{{R^3}} \over {{d^3}}}$$ [given, $$B \propto {{{R^m}} \over {{d^n}}}$$]

$$\therefore$$ m = 3, n = 3

content: For two types of magnetic materials A and B, variation of $$1\over\chi$$ ($$\chi$$ : susceptibility) versus temperature T is shown in the figure. Then,

options.content: A is diamagnetic and B is paramagnetic.
A is feromagnetic and B is diamagnetic.
A is paramagnetic and B is feromagnetic.
A is paramagnetic and B is diamagnetic.

explanation:
Since, susceptibility $$\chi $$ of diamagnetic material is independent of temperature.


Hence, $${1 \over \chi }$$ is also independent of temperature.

Therefore, material B is diamagnetic. For paramagnetic substance, susceptibility varies inversely as temperature.

i.e. $$\chi$$ $$\propto$$ $${1 \over T}$$ or $${1 \over \chi}$$ $$\propto$$ T

Therefore, material A is paramagnetic.

content: As shown in the figure, a single conducting wire is bent to form a loop in the form of a circle of radius r concentrically inside a square of side a, where a : r = 8 : $$\pi $$. A battery B drives a current through the wire. If the battery B and the gap G are of negligible sizes, determine the strength of magnetic field at the common centre O.
explanation:
According to given figure,


Given, a : r = 8 : $$\pi $$

$${a \over r} = {8 \over \pi } \Rightarrow r = {{\pi a} \over 8}$$ ...........(i)

Magnetic field due to square, $${B_1} = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {\pi {a \over 2}}} \times \sqrt 2 $$

outward

Magnetic field due to circular loop, $${B_2} = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {2r}}$$ ,

inward

Strength of magnetic field at the common centre O,

$$B = {{{\mu _0}I \times 8} \over {2\pi a}} - {{2\sqrt 2 {\mu _0}I} \over {\pi a}}$$

Substituting value of r from Eq (i), we get

$$B = {{{\mu _0}I \times 8} \over {2\pi a}} - {{2\sqrt 2 {\mu _0}I} \over {\pi a}}$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {\pi a}}(4 - 2\sqrt 2 )$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {\pi a}}2\sqrt 2 (\sqrt 2 - 1)$$

content: As shown in the figure, a wire is bent to form a D-shaped closed loop, carrying current I, where the curved part is a semi-circle of radius R. The loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field B, which is directed into the plane of the paper. The magnetic force felt by the closed loop is
explanation: A wire is bent to form a D-shaped loop carrying current I, where the curved part is semi-circle of radius R. The loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field B which is directed into the plane of the paper. As single current by flowing in the loop, so net magnetic force on a closed current loop in a uniform magnetic field B is zero.

content: To which of the following quantities, the radius of the circular path of a charged particle moving at right angles to a uniform magnetic field is directly proportional?
explanation:
If velocity of particle, v is perpendicular to magnetic field, B i.e. $$\theta$$ = 90$$^\circ$$, then particle will experience maximum magnetic force, i.e. Fmax = qvB. This force acts in a direction perpendicular to the motion of charged particle. Therefore the trajectory of the particle is a circle. In this case path of charged particle is circular and magnetic force provides the necessary centripetal force,

i.e. $$qvB = {{m{v^2}} \over r}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Radius of path, $$r = {{mv} \over {qB}}$$

$$r = {p \over {qB}}$$ [$$ \because $$ p = mv]

where, p = momentum of the particle

$$ \therefore $$ r $$ \propto $$ momentum

Hence, option (d) is correct.

content: An electric current 'I' enters and leaves a uniform circular wire of radius r through diametrically opposite points. A particle carrying a charge q moves along the axis of the circular wire with speed v. What is the magnetic force experienced by the particle when it passes through the centre of the circle?
explanation:
Given, electric current in circular wire = I

Radius of wire = r

Charge on particle = q

Speed of the particle = v

The given loop can be as shown in the figure below.


$$ \because $$ Net magnetic field at the centre = 0

So, magnetic force Fmagnetic = q(v $$ \times $$ B) = 0

So, the magnetic force experienced by the particle when it passes through the centre is F = 0

content: A current 'I' is flowing along an infinite, straight wire, in the positive Z-direction and the same current is flowing along a similar parallel wire 5 m apart, in the negative Z-direction. A point P is at a perpendicular distance 3m from the first wire and 4m from the second. What will be magnitude of the magnetic field B of P?
explanation:
According to the question,


Magnetic field due to first wire (A) is $${B_1} = {{{\mu _0} \times 1} \over {2\pi \times 3}}$$

Magnetic field due to second wire (B) is $${B_2} = {{{\mu _0} \times 1} \over {2\pi \times 4}}$$

Net magnitude of magnetic field $$B = \sqrt {B_1^2 + B_2^2} $$

$$B = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {2\pi }}\sqrt {{1 \over 9} + {1 \over {16}}} = {{{\mu _0}I \times 5} \over {2\pi \times 3 \times 4}}$$

Magnetic field $$B = {5 \over {24}} \times {{{\mu _0}I} \over \pi }$$

content:
A square conducting loop is placed near an infinitely long current carrying wire with one edge parallel to the wire as shown in the figure. If the current in the straight wire is suddenly halved, which of the following statements will be true?


"The loop will .........".
explanation:
As we know that, magnetic field due to a long wire at a distance x from it is given by $$B = {{{\mu _0}I} \over {2\pi x}}$$, where, I is the current flowing through the wire $$\beta \propto I$$

$$ \therefore $$ Magnetic flux associated with the square loop, $$\phi \propto \beta \propto I$$

Now, if the current increase, then $$\phi$$ also increases. Direction of long wire will be $$ \otimes $$

This means, magnetic field due to induced current will be opposite to the existing magnetic field, i.e. according to Lenz's law,

The induced current in the loop will be in the anti-clockwise direction. Now,


Since, wires attract each other, if current flowing through them is in same direction and repel each other, if currents are in opposite direction.

$$ \therefore $$ Part CD of the loop will experience a force of repulsion, whereas part AB will experience attraction. Parts BC and AD will not experience any force. Thus, the overall force will be a force of repulsion because AB is closer to straight. The force between two current carrying conductors is inversely proportional to the distance between them

$$F \propto {1 \over r}$$

$$ \because $$ $${r_1} < {r_2}$$

So, $${F_{CD}} > {F_{AB}}$$

$${F_{net}} = {F_{CD}} - {F_{AB}}$$

Hence, the loop will moves away from the wire.

content: A particle with charge q moves with a velocity v in a direction perpendicular to the directions of uniform electric and magnetic fields, E and B respectively, which are mutually perpendicular to each other. Which one of the following gives the condition for which the particle moves undeflected in its original trajectory?
explanation:
According to the question,

Charge on particle is = q

Velocity of particle = v

Due to uniform electric field,

electric force on particle

Felectric = qE ....(i)

Due to a uniform magnetic field,

,magnetic force on particle is given by,

Fmagnetic = q(v $$ \times $$ B) ....(ii)

When v is perpendicular to E and B, which are mutually perpendicular to each other,

Felectric = Fmagnetic

From Eqs. (i) and (ii)

qE = qvB

$$ \therefore $$ $$v = {E \over B}$$

So, $$v = {E \over B}$$ is the condition for which the particle moves undeflected in its original trajectory.

content: A proton of mass m moving with a speed v (< < c, velocity of light in vacuum) completes a circular orbit in time T in a uniform magnetic field. If the speed of the proton is increased to $$\sqrt 2 $$ v, what will be time needed to complete the circular orbit?
explanation:
$$ \because $$ When a charged particle moves at a circular path in uniform magnetic field, it time period is independent from its speed.

$$ \therefore $$ Time period of proton will not change with change in its speed.

content: A uniform current is flowing along the length of an infinite, straight, thin, hollow cylinder of radius R. The magnetic field B produced at a perpendicular distance d from the axis of the cylinder is plotted in a graph. Which of the following figures looks like the plot?
explanation:
$$ \because $$ Magnetic field due to a hollow cylinder of radius r.


$${B_{inside}} = 0$$ (for d < r)

$${B_{outside}} = {{{\mu _0}} \over {2\pi }}\,.\,{i \over d}$$ (for d $$ \ge $$ r)

So, graph between B and d.

content: A circular loop of radius r of conducting wire connected with a voltage source of zero internal resistance produces a magnetic field B at its centre. If instead, a circular loop of radius 2r, made of same material, having the same cross-section is connected to the same voltage source, what will be the magnetic field at its centre?
explanation:
$$B = {{{\mu _0}} \over 2}\,.\,{I \over r}$$

$$ \because $$ $${I_1} = {V \over {{R_1}}}$$

Case (I) $${I_1} = 2\pi r$$ (length of the loop),

$${R_1} = \rho {{{l_1}} \over A}$$ and $$B = {{{\mu _0}} \over 2}\,.\,{I \over r}$$ ....(i)

Case (ii) $${I_2} = 2\pi \,.\,2r = 2{l_1}$$

$${R_2} = \rho \,.\,{{{l_2}} \over A} = \rho \,.\,{{2{l_1}} \over A} = 2{R_1}$$

$${I_2} = {V \over {{R_2}}} = {V \over {2{R_1}}} = {{{I_1}} \over 2}$$

$${B_2} = {{{\mu _0}} \over 2}\,.\,{{{I_2}} \over {2r}} = {{{\mu _0}} \over 2}\,.\,{{{I_1}} \over {2\,.\,2r}}$$ ....(ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii)

$${{{B_2}} \over {{B_1}}} = {{{{{\mu _0}} \over 2}\,.\,{{{I_1}} \over {2\,.\,2r}}} \over {{{{\mu _0}} \over 2}\,.\,{{{I_1}} \over r}}} = {1 \over 4}$$

Magnetic field $${B_2} = {{{B_1}} \over 4}$$

content: A light charged particle is revolving in a circle of radius r in electrostatic attraction of a static heavy particle with opposite charge. How does the magnetic field B at the centre of the circle due to the moving charge depend on r?
explanation:
$${F_{centripetal}} = mr{\omega ^2} = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,.\,{{{q_1}{q_2}} \over {{r^2}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${\omega ^2} = {1 \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}m}}\,.\,{{{q_1}{q_2}} \over {{r^3}}} \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt {{{{q_1}{q_2}} \over {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}m{r^3}}}} $$

$$\omega \propto {1 \over {{r^{3/2}}}}$$ ....(i)

Current produced due to moving q1,

$$i = {\omega \over {2\pi }}{q_1}$$ ....(ii)

Thus, magnetic field due to motion of q1 in circular path at point O.

$$B = {{{\mu _0}} \over 2}\,.\,{i \over r} = {{{\mu _0}} \over 2}\,.\,{{\omega {q_1}} \over {2\pi r}}$$ [using Eq. (ii)]

Hence, $$B \propto {\omega \over r}$$

Magnetic field $$B \propto {1 \over {{r^{5/2}}}}$$ [using Eq. (i)]

content: The magnetic field due to a current in a straight wire segment of length L at a point on  its perpendicular bisector at a distance r(r >> L)

options.content: approaches a finite limit as r $$ \to $$ $$\infty $$

explanation:
By Biot-Savart's law, the magnetic field due to a current carrying wire is

$$B = {{{\mu _0}} \over {4\pi }}.{{Idl\sin \theta } \over {{r^2}}}$$

So, $$B \propto {1 \over {{r^2}}}$$

So, the magnetic field due to current decreases as inverse square of distance of the point of observation.

content: The magnets of two suspended coil galvanometers are of the same strength so that they produce identical uniform magnetic fields in the region of the coils. The coil of the first one is in the shape of a square of side a and that of the second one is circular of radius $${a \over {\sqrt \pi  }}$$. When the same current is passed through the coils, the ratio of the torque experienced by the first coil to that experienced by the second one is
explanation:
In galvanometer I $$ \propto $$ $$\theta$$ = G$$\theta$$

Where, $$G = {k \over {NAB}}$$

When coil is in square of shape,

$$G = {k \over {NAB}} = {k \over {N{a^2}B}}$$ ..... (i)

When coil is in circular shape of radius $$a/\sqrt \pi $$

$$G = {k \over {NAB}} = {k \over {N\pi {r^2}.B}}$$

$$ = {k \over {N\pi {{\left( {{a \over {\sqrt \pi }}} \right)}^2}.B}} = {k \over {N{a^2}B}}$$ .... (ii)

So, value of G is same for both type of shape of coil. So, the torque e = NBIA sin$$\theta $$ will be same in both the case

e : e = 1 : 1

content: A proton is moving with a uniform velocity of $${{{10}^6}}$$ ms<sup>$$-$$1</sup> along the Y-axis, under the joint action of a magnetic field along Z-axis and an electric field of magnitude 2 $$ \times $$ 10<sup>4</sup> Vm<sup>$$-$$1</sup> along the negative X-axis. If the electric field is switched off, the proton starts moving in a circle. The radius of the circle is nearly (given : $${e \over m}$$ ratio for proton $$ \approx $$ 10<sup>8</sup> Ckg<sup>$$-$$1</sup>)
explanation:
Velocity of proton = 106 m/s along y-direction


Electric field = 2 $$ \times $$ 104 V/m

$${e \over m}$$ for proton = 10$$-$$8 C/kg

The magnetic field,

$$B = {E \over v}$$ ($$ \because $$ $${q_p}{v_B} = {q_p}E$$)

$$ = {{2 \times {{10}^4}} \over {{{10}^6}}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}T$$

The radius of circular path

$$\gamma = {{mv} \over {{q_p}.B}} = {{{{10}^{ - 8}} \times {{10}^6}} \over {2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} = 0.5$$

content: A particle with charge e and mass m, moving along the X-axis with a uniform speed u, enters a region where a uniform electric field E is acting along the Y-axis. The particle starts to move in a parabola. Its focal length (neglecting any effect of gravity) is
explanation:
From parabola


$$y = {1 \over 2} \times \left[ {{{Ee} \over m}} \right] \times {{{X^2}} \over {{u^2}}}$$

$$ = {{E.\,e} \over {2m{u^2}}}.\,{X^2}$$

As $$X = 4ay$$ (for parabola)

$$ \therefore $$ $${X^2} = {{2m{u^2}} \over {E.e}}.y$$

$$a = {{2m{u^2}} \over {4E.\,e}} = {{m{u^2}} \over {2E.\,e}}$$

content: An electron enters an electric field having intensity $$E = 3\widehat i + 6\widehat j + 2\widehat k$$ Vm<sup>$$-$$1</sup> and magnetic field having induction $$B = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j$$ T with a velocity $$v = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j$$ ms<sup>$$-$$1</sup>. The magnitude of the force acting on the electron is (Given, $$e =  - 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}$$ C)
explanation:
Given,

$$E = 3\widehat i + 6\widehat j + 2\widehat k$$ V/m

$$B = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat jT$$

$$v = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j$$ m/s

$$e = - 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}$$C

The magnitude of electric field

$$|E|\, = \sqrt {{{(3)}^2} + {{(6)}^2} + {{(2)}^2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {9 + 36 + 4} $$

$$ = \sqrt {49} = 7$$

The force acting on electron due to electric field

$$F = qE$$

$$|F|\, = \,|q||E|$$

$$ = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 7N$$

$$ = 11.2 \times {10^{ - 19}}N$$

$$ = 1.12 \times {10^{ - 18}}N$$

Alternate Method

$$B||v$$ Magnetic field is parallel to velocity of electron. $$v||B$$ Magnetic force

$${F_m} = q(v \times B)$$

$$ = qvB\sin \theta $$ ($$ \because $$ $$\theta$$ = 0)

$$ = qvB\sin 0$$

$$ = 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ Net force $$F = {F_e} + {F_m}$$

$$ = (1.12 \times {10^{ - 18}} + 0)N$$

$$ = 1.12 \times {10^{ - 18}}N$$

content: As shown in figure, a rectangular loop of length 'a' and width 'b' and made of a conducting material of uniform cross-section is kept in a horizontal plane where a uniform magnetic field of intensity B is acting vertically downward. Resistance per unit length of the loop is $$\lambda$$ $$\Omega$$/m. If the loop is pulled with uniform velocity 'v' in horizontal direction, which of the following statement is/are true?

options.content: Current will be in clockwise direction, looking from the top.
V<sub>P</sub> $$-$$ V<sub>S</sub> = V<sub>Q</sub> $$-$$ V<sub>R</sub>, where V is the potential.
There cannot be any induction in part SR.

explanation:
Given, resistance per unit length of loop

$$ = \lambda \,\Omega /m$$

(a) Current in the loop, $$I = {E \over R}$$

$$I = {{Blv} \over R}$$ ($$\because$$ emf $$E = Blv$$) ......... (i)

We know that,

$${R \over {2(a + b)}} = \lambda \Rightarrow R = \lambda (2a + 2b)$$ .......... (ii)

Using Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$I = {{Bbv} \over {\lambda (2a + 2b)}}$$

So, option (a) is correct.

(b) Using right hand thumb rule, direction of current in the loop will be clockwise, looking from the top.

But as the flux is changing, so according to Lenz's law, direction of current will be anti-clockwise.

So, option (b) is incorrect.

(c) As the arm PS and QR has same potential, then $${V_P} - {V_S} = {V_Q} - {V_R}$$ is also same.

So, option (c) is correct.

(d) As the arm SR is parallel to the velocity. So, there will not be any current or induction.

So, option (d) is correct.

content: A small bar magnet of dipole moment M is moving with speed v along x-direction towards a small closed circular conducting loop of radius a with its centre O at x = 0 (see figure). Assume x >> a and the coil has a resistance R. Then, which of the following statements is/are true?

options.content: Magnetic field at the centre O of the circular coil due to the bar magnet is $${M \over {{x^3}}}$$
The magnetic moment $$\mu$$ due to induced current in the coil is proportional to a<sup>4</sup>
The heat produced is proportional to $${1 \over {{x^6}}}$$

explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre O of the circular coil due to bar magnet (axial position)

$$B = {{{\mu _0}} \over {4\pi }}.{{2M} \over {{x^3}}}$$

Hence, option (a) is not true.

Induced emf, $$e = - {{d\phi } \over {dt}} = - {d \over {dt}}.\,BA$$

$$ = - {d \over {dt}}.\,{{{\mu _0}} \over {4\pi }}\,.\,{{2M} \over {{x^3}}}\pi {a^2}$$

$$ = - {{{\mu _0}M{a^2}} \over 2}{d \over {dt}}.\,{1 \over {{x^3}}}$$

$$ = - {\mu _0}M{a^2}\,.\,\left( {{{ - 3} \over {{x^4}}}} \right){{dx} \over {dt}}$$

$$e = {{3{\mu _0}M{a^2}} \over {{x^4}}}.\,{{dx} \over {dt}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow e \propto {1 \over {{x^4}}}$$

Hence, option (b) is true.

Induced current, $$I = {e \over R} = {{3{\mu _0}M{a^2}} \over {{x^4}R}}{{dx} \over {dt}}$$ ..... (i)

$$\therefore$$ Magnetic moment, $$\mu = IA = {{3{\mu _0}M{a^2}} \over {{x^4}R}}{{dx} \over {dt}}\pi {a^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \mu = {{3\pi {\mu _0}M} \over {{x^4}R}}{{dx} \over {dt}}({a^4})$$

$$\mu \propto {a^4}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ Hence, option (c) is true.

Net heat produced,

H $$\propto$$ I2

$$\Rightarrow$$ $$H \propto {\left( {{1 \over {{x^4}}}} \right)^2}$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$ \Rightarrow H \propto {1 \over {{x^8}}}$$

Hence, option (d) is not true.

content: A charged particle moves with constant velocity in a region, where no effect of gravity is felt but an electrostatic field E together with a magnetic field B may be present. Then, which of the following cases are possible?
explanation:
It is given that the charged particle is moving with constant velocity in a region of no gravity, so following cases are possible

(i) The particle may move in a straight line in any direction, when E = 0 and B = 0.

(ii) The particle may move in a circle with constant velocity. The centripetal force for circular motion is provided by magnetic field. So, E = 0 and B $$ e $$ 0.

(iii) The particle may move in a helical path with constant velocity. For helical motion, the condition is that both fields must be present i.e. E $$ e $$ 0 and B $$ e $$ 0.

content: A metallic loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field B with the plane of the loop perpendicular to B. Under which condition(s) given an emf will be induced in the loop? "If the loop is ............"

options.content: moved along the direction of B.
rotated about one of its diameters

explanation:
According to the question,


Given, A metallic loop is placed in uniform magnetic field B. Magnetic field B and metallic loop are perpendicular to each other. Then, if metallic loop is moved along B or rotated about its own axis, the net flux associated with it remains constant. Thus, no emf will be induced in these cases. However, when the loop is squeezed to a smaller area or rotated about one of its diameters. Then its flux changes. Thus, emf is induced. So, option (b) and (d) both are correct.

content: Two long parallel wires separated by 0.1 m carry currents of 1A and 2A, respectively in opposite directions. A third current-carrying wire parallel to both of them is placed in the same plane such that it feels no net magnetic force. It is placed at a distance of

options.content: 0.5 m from the 1st wire, towards the 2nd wire
0.2 m from the 1st wire, towards the 2nd wire.
0.1 m from the 1st wire, away from the 2nd wire
0.2 m from the 1st wire, away from the 2nd wire.

explanation:
According to question, the third wire should not feel magnetic force due to wire (1) and (2). It can be only possible in the condition that fields due to wire (1) and (2) must act opposite to each other and must be equal. The magnetic field due to long straight wire,

$$B = {{{\mu _0}.\,I} \over {2\pi x}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${B_1} = {B_2}$$

$${{{\mu _0}{i_1}} \over {2\pi x}} = {{{\mu _0}{i_2}} \over {2\pi (0.1 + x)}}$$

Here $${i_1} = 1A$$

$${i_2} = 2A$$

$${{{\mu _0} \times 1} \over {2\pi x}} = {{{\mu _0} \times 2} \over {2\pi (0.1 + x)}}$$

2x = (0 . 1 + x)

x = 0.1 m

content: If $$\chi $$ stands for the magnetic susceptibility of a substance, $$\mu$$ for its magnetic permeability and $$\mu$$<sub>0</sub> for the permeability of free space, then
explanation:
We know that magnetic susceptibility

$$\chi $$ = $$\mu$$r $$-$$ 1

and $${\mu _r} = {\mu \over {{\mu _0}}}$$

= Relative permeability

For paramagnetic substance,

$$\chi $$ > 0

$$ \therefore $$ $$\mu$$r > 1

$$ \therefore $$ u > $$\mu$$0

For diamagnetic substance,

$$\chi $$ < 0

$$ \therefore $$ $$\mu$$r < 1

$$ \therefore $$ u < $$\mu$$0

For ferromagnetic substance,

$$\chi $$ > > 1

$$\mu$$ > > M0

If 0 < $$\mu$$ < $$\mu$$0

Then substance will not be paramagnetic. Hence option (a) is incorrect and option (b) and (d) are correct.

content: A rectangular coil carrying-current is placed in a non-uniform magnetic field. On that coil, the total
explanation:
Current-carrying rectangular coil is placed in a non-uniform magnetic field. So, the force on each arm of the coil will different giving the resultant force as non-zero.

Thus, option (a) is correct.

Also, the torque acting on current-carrying coil in non-uniform magnetic field will be non-zero.

Thus, option (d) is also correct.
